title: Teaching and Supervision
text:
I teach within the Department of Political Science at the University of Gothenburg at the Bachelors and Masters levels. I also teach interdisciplinary courses in the Masters Program Sea and Society affiliated with Department of Marine Sciences at the Faculty of Science and Technology.




Bachelor’s level, Department of Political Science, University of Gothenburg

Environmental Ethics — Lecture and seminar leader (Fall 2026)




Master’s level, Department of Marine Sciences, University of Gothenburg

Politics of Climate Change (Spring 2026)





I supervise master’s theses within political science, primarily in the areas of environmental politics and public opinion.

title: Lauren Yehle
text:
This section needs to be updated… I am a PhD student in Political Science at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden. My research examines the political feasibility of environmental policy, with a particular focus on how compensation can increase support for ambitious climate action.
My doctoral research investigates compensation from both elite and public perspectives. One project examines whether compensating industries that oppose climate policy can improve the political feasibility of climate reforms. A second explores how perceptions of deservingness shape public support for compensating groups that may bear the costs of environmental policy, such as unemployed workers and affected local communities.
Alongside my dissertation, I work with Professor Aksel Sundström on the ERC-funded project FEM-Lead, conducting a meta-analysis of how natural disasters influence politically relevant outcomes, with a focus on electoral incumbency and institutional trust.
Before beginning my PhD in 2025, I worked for nearly three years as a research assistant in the Departments of Political Science and Economics at the University of Gothenburg. I received an MSc in Political Science, specializing in Environmental Governance and Human Behavior, from the University of Gothenburg in 2023 (thesis awarded Pass with Distinction). I earned a BA in Political Science, with minors in Economics and History, from the University of Denver in 2019, graduating summa cum laude and as a member of Phi Beta Kappa.
More broadly, my research interests include environmental politics, public opinion, environmental governance, and collective action. I am an organizer with the Centre for Environmental Political Studies (CEPS) at the University of Gothenburg and have contributed to organizing several research workshops, including Closing the Legitimacy Gap at NESS 2026.
Please feel free to get in touch if you would like to discuss my research or potential collaborations.

title: Home
text:
I am a PhD student in Political Science at the University of Gothenburg. I study the political feasibility of environmental policy. Specifically, I investigate how compensation can make ambitious climate policies more politically acceptable among both organized interests and the public.
My work combines public opinion research with political economy to understand how governments can design environmentally effective and politically feasible policies. Alongside my dissertation, I work on the ERC-funded project Fem-LEAD, studying how natural disasters shape political behavior and institutional trust.

title: Research
text:
My research examines the political feasibility of environmental policy, with a particular focus on how compensation can facilitate ambitious climate action. Broadly, I study how governments can design environmental policies that are both effective and politically acceptable.
My work combines theories of political behavior, public opinion, and environmental governance to understand how citizens, organized interests, and policymakers respond to environmental policy.




My dissertation investigates the political feasibility of compensating those who bear the costs of environmental policy.
The first paper examines whether compensating industries that oppose climate policy can increase the likelihood that ambitious climate policies are passed.
The second paper explores how perceptions of deservingness shape public willingness to compensate groups that may lose from environmental policy, including unemployed workers and affected local communities.



Alongside my dissertation, I contribute to the ERC-funded project Fem-LEAD with Professor Aksel Sundström. My current work synthesizes evidence on how natural disasters influence politically relevant outcomes through systematic review and meta-analysis, with a focus on electoral incumbency and political trust.
Learn more about the project on the Fem-LEAD website.





Environmental politics
Public opinion
Climate and Biodiversity Loss policy acceptance
Environmental governance
Collective action
Political behavior
Political economy of the green transition




My work primarily employs quantitative methods, including survey experiments, causal inference, systematic reviews, and meta-analysis.
